comments:
Summary:
This paper reports practical experience and engineering results for performing data-intensive scientific processing in the commercial cloud. The authors describe architectural considerations, introduce a custom FUSE-based virtual filesystem (festivus) on top of cloud object storage, present micro- and macro-benchmarks (including STREAM, NAS, LINPACK comparisons and end-to-end bandwidth tests), and demonstrate large-scale applications: processing ~1 PB of Landsat/MODIS imagery quickly, generating a global cloud-free composite, and producing field segmentation results. They report very large aggregate read bandwidth (231 GB/s across 512 GCE nodes) and strong cost/performance arguments (e.g. LINPACK teraflop cost comparisons and the 100k CPU-hour composite at ~$1000).

Strengths:
- The work addresses an important practical problem (bringing POSIX-like access and high-performance I/O semantics to cloud object stores) that is highly relevant to scientific remote sensing and data-intensive HPC in the cloud.
- The festivus approach and associated engineering optimizations (e.g., tuning kernel FUSE parameters, use of Redis for metadata) are concrete and appear to enable impressive aggregate throughput and scalable processing.
- The paper provides multiple quantitative comparisons to historical HPC systems and to alternative implementations (gcsfuse), and includes realistic application-level demonstrations (petabyte processing, composite imagery, segmentation) that showcase end-to-end feasibility.
- Cost and operational comparisons (pre-emptible instances, programmer cost amortization) are compelling and useful for practitioners.

Weaknesses / Suggestions for improvement:
- Experimental details could be expanded to improve reproducibility and to strengthen claims. For example: precise benchmark methodologies (how data were distributed, how reads overlapped with computation, network topology/placement of VMs relative to storage buckets), timing breakdowns (I/O vs compute), and any variability/error bars are not presented. Including these would clarify the sources of the reported bandwidth and where bottlenecks appear.
- The festivus implementation requires kernel parameter changes (increasing FUSE_MAX_PAGES_PER_REQ, VM_MAX_READAHEAD) and a kernel build. This reduces portability and ease of deployment; the paper should discuss operational trade-offs and deployment burden in more detail (e.g., can festivus be deployed on managed/locked environments, is the kernel modification required universally, and any security/maintenance implications).
- The metadata server (Redis) is noted as shared among instances. The paper should discuss metadata scalability limits, failure modes, consistency guarantees, and whether Redis is a single point of failure or sharded/replicated in production.
- The use of pre-emptible nodes is an important economic lever but also affects job reliability. The manuscript should describe how pre-emption is handled (task restart/checkpointing, lost work fraction) and how that affects end-to-end cost and wall-clock time.
- Comparisons to gcsfuse are useful but appear limited to a few cases. Additional head-to-head evaluations across a wider set of file sizes, access patterns, and concurrency levels would strengthen the claim that festivus provides real advantages.
- Some claims (e.g., 75x less CPU-hours than Google Maps computation) are interesting but would benefit from more explicit methodology and assumptions behind the estimate.

Minor / Editorial:
- A few duplicated/repeated text fragments appear in the provided manuscript copy; ensure the final version is free of such duplication.
- Provide pointers to the festivus source or deployment instructions, benchmark scripts, and data-processing pipelines to aid reproducibility and adoption.

Overall recommendation:
This paper presents useful, well-motivated engineering work demonstrating that cloud architectures can support data-intensive scientific processing at very large scale and relatively low cost. With modest additions to experimental detail and discussion of operational trade-offs, the manuscript will be a valuable resource for the community. I recommend acceptance (strongly positive), subject to clarifications and expanded methodological detail.
